original_abstract: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of cosinor-based techniques tailored for time-series analysis in chronobiology. Framed as a regression problem, the method offers the distinct advantage of being applicable to non-equidistant data. A significant benefit of this approach is the ability to derive precise confidence intervals for rhythmic parameters with known periods, leveraging the least squares procedure and highlighting the value of incorporating prior external information. While originally designed to handle short and sparse datasets, the extended cosinor has evolved into a robust tool for analyzing long-term time series, facilitating both rhythm detection and parameter estimation. We examine the fundamental assumptions of the cosinor model, provide diagnostic tools to verify them, and present strategies for managing non-stationary data. Through various illustrative examples, we demonstrate the versatility of these methods, extending their application from traditional circadian rhythm research to the mapping of complex, broad-scale time structures known as chronomes.
humanized_abstract: This paper is a review of the methodologies based on the cosinor procedure, which have been devised for the analysis of time series in Chronobiology. The procedure is formulated as a regression problem where it is shown that the method is generally applicable to data with a non uniform distribution in time. Through the least squares procedure, it is also possible to obtain the confidence intervals of the amplitudes, mean values and acrophases of the rhythms whose periods are a priori known. Although the Extended Cosinor Model was developed to work with short and sparse time series, it has proved to be also very useful for the analysis of longer time series with the aim of detecting the rhythms present in these data and estimating their characteristics. Basic hypotheses of the Cosinor Model are analyzed, as well as the procedures for checking them and for facing non-stationarity in the data. The suitability of these methods for the analysis of the different data sets in Chronobiology is shown, ranging from the usual studies on circadian rhythms to the characterization of the so-called chronomes, i.e., the representation of complex time patterns covering large scales of time.
